content.text: Why GenAI Needs Its Own Severity Matrix

Standard web service severity matrices classify incidents along two axes: user impact (how many users are affected) and functional impact (complete outage vs. degradation). GenAI systems require a third axis:

. A GenAI service can be operationally healthy — returning 200 responses at normal latency with zero errors — while simultaneously producing harmful, biased, or factually incorrect outputs at scale. This is a high-severity incident by any reasonable definition, but a standard web service severity matrix would classify it as no incident at all.

The matrix must also handle provider incidents differently from application incidents. When OpenAI or Anthropic experiences an outage, the root cause and resolution path are entirely outside the engineering team's control. The severity classification should remain the same (a 100% LLM API error rate is SEV 1 regardless of cause), but the incident response playbook differs: the mitigation is a provider failover or graceful degradation, not a code fix.

attrs.alt: GenAI Incident Severity Matrix — functional degradation vs user impact scope

content.text: Complete GenAI service outage affecting all users (> 5% error rate platform-wide for > 5 minutes)
Any safety or harmful output event regardless of scope — detected harmful content, prompt injection with user data exfiltration, or content policy violation at scale
Any compliance breach: PII leak in logs, PHI reaching an uncovered LLM API, PCI card data in prompts
LLM provider outage with no available fallback model, causing > 50% of requests to fail
Cost runaway: spend rate > 10× normal for > 10 minutes with no known cause
Error rate > 5% for a specific model or provider affecting a significant user segment (> 20% of users)
P99 latency > 30 seconds platform-wide for > 10 minutes (streaming responses timing out before completion)
Rate limiting affecting > 10% of traffic with no available quota expansion
Quality regression > 20% drop in eval score for a primary feature, confirmed by automated eval run
Observability platform outage — Loki/Grafana/Alertmanager down during an active incident investigation
Error rate > 5% for a single model tier or feature, affecting < 20% of traffic
P99 latency > 10 seconds for a specific model or feature
Streaming broken for a subset of models or client types
Rate limiting affecting 5–10% of traffic with a known quota expansion path
Quality regression 10–20% in eval score for a secondary feature
Cost spend 3–10× normal for > 30 minutes (investigate but not emergency)

, regardless of scope. One confirmed harmful output to one user is SEV 1 if it indicates a systematic failure (prompt injection vulnerability, content filter bypass, jailbreak at scale). The reasoning: the blast radius is unknowable at the time of detection. A single logged case of harmful output often means thousands of unlogged cases.

The legal and regulatory consequences of under-responding to a safety incident are asymmetric: responding at SEV 1 to something that turns out to be a false positive costs one incident response session. Under-responding to a real harmful output event at SEV 3 can result in regulatory action, user harm, and reputational damage that dwarfs the incident response cost. When in doubt, classify up.
Model produces instructions for self-harm in response to normal prompts
User-provided prompt causes system to output other users' data
Disable feature; rotate API keys; notify security
A bypass technique that causes content_filter=false on inputs that should be filtered
Block endpoint; page security; investigate scope
User prompt text logged to accessible Loki tenant
Lock down log tenant; notify DPO; scope audit
Healthcare user submitted patient data; no BAA with provider
Disable feature; notify legal; begin BAA process or terminate data flow
EU AI Act audit log not retained; PCI card data in prompt
Stop data flow; notify compliance officer; document timeline

Several incident patterns are unique to GenAI systems and do not map cleanly onto web service classifications. The following table documents common scenarios with their correct severity classification and the key distinguishing factor.
Provider API returns 429 rate limit for 5% of requests
Provider API returns 429 for 40% of requests, no quota increase available
Provider API returns 503 for 100% of requests
Complete outage, regardless of provider cause
Model returns empty completions (finish_reason=length) for long prompts
Functional degradation for a subset of use cases
Eval score drops 5% over 7 days (slow drift)
No user impact yet; investigation needed
Eval score drops 25% overnight after model version change
Likely model deployment issue; significant quality impact
Token cost per request increases 3× without traffic change
Investigate prompt change or model version; not emergency
Token cost per request increases 50× — possible runaway agent loop
Financial risk and potential infinite loop; requires immediate stop
Content filter fires on previously-safe inputs after model update
If systematic: SEV 2. If combined with harmful outputs: SEV 1
User experience degradation but service functional
Streaming completions silently truncated at 512 tokens
Silent data loss; users receiving incomplete responses

from enum import Enum

class Severity(Enum):
    SEV1 = 1
    SEV2 = 2
    SEV3 = 3
    SEV4 = 4
    SEV5 = 5

def classify_genai_incident(
    safety_or_compliance_involved: bool,
    error_rate_pct: float,       # 0–100
    affected_traffic_pct: float, # 0–100, fraction of total requests affected
    p99_latency_s: float,        # seconds
    quality_drop_pct: float,     # 0–100, eval score reduction
    cost_multiplier: float,      # current spend / normal spend
) -> tuple[Severity, str]:
    """
    Returns (severity, reason) for a GenAI incident.
    Use the most severe classification when multiple criteria match.
    """
    # Safety/compliance always SEV1
    if safety_or_compliance_involved:
        return Severity.SEV1, "Safety or compliance incident — always SEV1 regardless of scope"

    # Cost runaway: stop runaway agents
    if cost_multiplier >= 10:
        return Severity.SEV1, f"Cost runaway: {cost_multiplier:.0f}× normal spend"
    if cost_multiplier >= 3:
        return Severity.SEV3, f"Cost anomaly: {cost_multiplier:.0f}× normal spend, investigate"

    # Complete outage
    if error_rate_pct > 5 and affected_traffic_pct > 80:
        return Severity.SEV1, f"Platform outage: {error_rate_pct:.0f}% errors across {affected_traffic_pct:.0f}% of traffic"

    # High error rate, significant scope
    if error_rate_pct > 5 and affected_traffic_pct > 20:
        return Severity.SEV2, f"High error rate: {error_rate_pct:.0f}% errors, {affected_traffic_pct:.0f}% of traffic"

    # Medium error rate or limited scope
    if error_rate_pct > 5:
        return Severity.SEV3, f"Elevated error rate: {error_rate_pct:.0f}% errors, limited scope"

    # Severe latency platform-wide
    if p99_latency_s > 30 and affected_traffic_pct > 50:
        return Severity.SEV2, f"Severe latency: P99 {p99_latency_s:.0f}s platform-wide"

    if p99_latency_s > 10:
        return Severity.SEV3, f"Elevated latency: P99 {p99_latency_s:.0f}s"

    # Quality regression
    if quality_drop_pct > 20:
        return Severity.SEV2, f"Major quality regression: {quality_drop_pct:.0f}% eval score drop"
    if quality_drop_pct > 10:
        return Severity.SEV3, f"Quality regression: {quality_drop_pct:.0f}% eval score drop"
    if quality_drop_pct > 5:
        return Severity.SEV4, f"Minor quality drift: {quality_drop_pct:.0f}% eval score drop"

    return Severity.SEV5, "No significant impact detected; monitoring"

# Examples
cases = [
    dict(safety_or_compliance_involved=True, error_rate_pct=0, affected_traffic_pct=0.1, p99_latency_s=1, quality_drop_pct=0, cost_multiplier=1),
    dict(safety_or_compliance_involved=False, error_rate_pct=80, affected_traffic_pct=100, p99_latency_s=5, quality_drop_pct=0, cost_multiplier=1),
    dict(safety_or_compliance_involved=False, error_rate_pct=0, affected_traffic_pct=0, p99_latency_s=1, quality_drop_pct=0, cost_multiplier=15),
    dict(safety_or_compliance_involved=False, error_rate_pct=6, affected_traffic_pct=30, p99_latency_s=2, quality_drop_pct=0, cost_multiplier=1),
]
for c in cases:
    sev, reason = classify_genai_incident(**c)
    print(f"{sev.name}: {reason}")
# SEV1: Safety or compliance incident — always SEV1 regardless of scope
# SEV1: Platform outage: 80% errors across 100% of traffic
# SEV1: Cost runaway: 15× normal spend
# SEV2: High error rate: 6% errors, 30% of traffic

The classification above does not change based on whether the root cause is in the application code or a third-party LLM provider. However, the response playbook differs significantly. Provider incidents require the on-call to attempt failover first (switch to an alternate model or provider) before investigating application code. Application incidents require tracing through the request pipeline to find the regression.
All models from one provider erroring
Rarely application (would need to break all models simultaneously)
Check recent config changes for that model
Error rate correlated with deployment timestamp
Error rate affects only one feature/team
Latency increase correlated with token count
Check for prompt bloat from recent change
Eval score drop with no infrastructure change
Check if provider silently updated model
Check for prompt template change adding tokens

Manual severity classification during a 3 AM incident is error-prone. Alerts should carry a pre-computed severity label derived from the same thresholds used in the classification decision tree, so the on-call engineer receives a page that already says
rather than having to evaluate conditions under pressure.

# Prometheus alert rules with severity pre-classified
groups:
- name: genai-incident-classification
  rules:

  # SEV 1: Platform-wide outage
  - alert: GenAISev1PlatformOutage
    expr: |
      (
        sum(rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[5m]))
        / sum(rate(genai_requests_total[5m]))
      ) > 0.05
      and
      sum(rate(genai_requests_total[5m])) > 100  # avoid false positive at low traffic
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: sev1
      team: platform
      page: "true"
    annotations:
      summary: "SEV1: GenAI platform error rate > 5% for 5m"
      runbook_url: "https://runbooks.example.com/sev1-platform-outage"

  # SEV 1: Cost runaway
  - alert: GenAISev1CostRunaway
    expr: |
      rate(genai_cost_usd_total[10m]) * 3600
      > on() group_left() (genai_cost_baseline_hourly * 10)
    for: 10m
    labels:
      severity: sev1
      team: platform
      page: "true"
    annotations:
      summary: "SEV1: GenAI cost runaway — > 10× baseline spend rate"
      runbook_url: "https://runbooks.example.com/sev1-cost-runaway"

  # SEV 2: High error rate limited scope
  - alert: GenAISev2ModelErrorRate
    expr: |
      (
        sum by (model) (rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[5m]))
        / sum by (model) (rate(genai_requests_total[5m]))
      ) > 0.05
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: sev2
      page: "true"
    annotations:
      summary: "SEV2: Model {{ $labels.model }} error rate > 5%"

  # SEV 2: Severe latency
  - alert: GenAISev2SevereLatency
    expr: |
      histogram_quantile(0.99,
        sum(rate(genai_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
      ) > 30
    for: 10m
    labels:
      severity: sev2
      page: "true"
    annotations:
      summary: "SEV2: GenAI P99 latency > 30s platform-wide"

  # SEV 3: Quality regression
  - alert: GenAISev3QualityRegression
    expr: |
      (
        avg_over_time(genai_eval_score[1h])
        - avg_over_time(genai_eval_score[24h] offset 1d)
      ) / avg_over_time(genai_eval_score[24h] offset 1d) < -0.10
    for: 30m
    labels:
      severity: sev3
      page: "false"   # business hours ticket, no page
    annotations:
      summary: "SEV3: Eval score dropped > 10% vs yesterday"

Each severity level has a defined escalation path that specifies who is notified, in what order, and via what channel. These paths must be documented and tested in game days so that on-call engineers do not have to look them up during an incident.
Escalate to secondary; page engineering director
Engineering VP + Legal within 30 min; status page update

is the window between initial page and escalation. If the primary on-call does not acknowledge the page within 15 minutes, PagerDuty automatically escalates to the secondary and pages the engineering director. This prevents a silent failure where the primary on-call is unavailable and the incident proceeds unacknowledged.
SLO Error Budget Burn as a Severity Signal

Multi-window multi-burn-rate (MWMBR) SLO alerts provide a severity signal that is calibrated to the rate at which the error budget is being consumed — not just the raw error rate. This is particularly useful for GenAI quality SLOs where a 2% quality regression might not trigger a raw error rate alert but could exhaust the monthly error budget within hours if sustained.

# SLO-based severity: burn rate drives classification
# Assumes 99.5% availability SLO with 30-day window
# Error budget = 0.5% × 30d × 86400s = 12,960 seconds of errors

- alert: GenAISLOCriticalBurnRate   # exhausts budget in < 1 hour
  expr: |
    (
      rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[5m]) /
      rate(genai_requests_total[5m])
    ) > (14.4 * 0.005)  # 14.4× burn = budget gone in 1h
    and
    (
      rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[1h]) /
      rate(genai_requests_total[1h])
    ) > (14.4 * 0.005)
  labels:
    severity: sev1
    slo_burn: critical

- alert: GenAISLOHighBurnRate       # exhausts budget in < 6 hours
  expr: |
    (
      rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[30m]) /
      rate(genai_requests_total[30m])
    ) > (6 * 0.005)
    and
    (
      rate(genai_requests_total{status="error"}[6h]) /
      rate(genai_requests_total[6h])
    ) > (6 * 0.005)
  labels:
    severity: sev2
    slo_burn: high

The advantage of burn-rate alerts over raw error rate alerts is that they adapt to traffic volume: a 10% error rate on 10 requests per second is far less severe than a 10% error rate on 10,000 requests per second, and burn-rate alerts reflect this correctly.
Worked Example: Classifying a Real Incident
. The on-call engineer receives the page and begins triage. Here is how the classification evolves as more information becomes available:
Check provider status page: OpenAI reports gpt-4o degradation
Post mitigation to incident channel; keep monitoring
gpt-4o error rate normalised; provider marks incident resolved
Review: 9 minutes at > 5% error rate for gpt-4o users
Document in incident log; check if SLO budget impacted

Note the downgrade at 02:23: once the failover mitigation is in place and the user-facing error rate drops below threshold, the incident classification can be reduced. Classification reflects current impact, not worst-case historical impact. However, the post-mortem should document the peak severity so the incident count by severity level reflects the true incident history.

GenAI incident severity classification requires extending the standard web service matrix with two GenAI-specific dimensions: output quality regression (where a service can be operationally healthy while producing harmful content) and safety/compliance events (which are always SEV 1 regardless of scope). The five-level matrix maps functional degradation severity against user impact scope, with the safety column always resolving to SEV 1 and triggering legal notification. The key classification thresholds are: > 5% error rate across > 80% of traffic = SEV 1; > 5% error rate in significant scope = SEV 2; isolated errors or moderate latency = SEV 3; slow quality drift = SEV 4. Cost anomalies at > 10× normal spend classify as SEV 1 due to the risk of runaway agent loops. Provider versus application root cause does not change the severity, but it changes the response playbook: provider incidents start with failover attempts; application incidents start with diff review.
